key_insight: The paper replaces algebraic residual-based sparse approximate inverse tuning with spectral optimization of a fixed-pattern factor $G$ so that the preconditioned operator $P(G)=GAG^T$ has eigenvalues clustered near the ideal values.
engineering_relevance: This can yield stronger preconditioners for large sparse symmetric systems, especially indefinite saddle-point problems common in finite-element simulation and optimization.
method: Optimize admissible entries of a fixed sparsity factor using spectral objectives instead of Frobenius-residual criteria.
Use Lanczos to estimate the spectral loss and obtain projected Krylov support-gradients for only the allowed nonzeros.
Implement a detached Rayleigh surrogate so gradients avoid backpropagating through the Lanczos recurrence.
evidence_signal: Experiments on finite-element test problems reported improved fixed-support preconditioners, with particularly strong gains on symmetric indefinite saddle-point systems, and also included a graph neural network for predicting factor entries across related matrices.
watch_item: The approach depends on approximate Lanczos/Krylov estimates and a fixed sparsity pattern, so performance and stability may be sensitive to support choice and surrogate-gradient accuracy.
paper_title: Factored Sparse Approximate Inverse Preconditioning via Spectral Optimization
